title: Mark Driscoll's Second Act Looks Exactly Like His First Act
teaser: Mark Driscoll resigned from Mars Hill Church in 2014 amid documented findings of bullying, plagiarism, and financial misconduct. He then founded a new church in Scottsdale, where former members and staff say the exact same thing happened again.
body:
There is a particular kind of accountability failure that occurs not when someone makes a mistake, but when someone makes the same mistake twice, at scale, with a fresh congregation that had no reason to know better. The Atlantic's 2021 examination of Mark Driscoll lays out this failure with the kind of patience the subject himself never appeared to extend to his subordinates. The story is not complicated. The story is, in fact, almost tediously consistent.

Driscoll built Mars Hill Church in Seattle into a congregation of approximately 15,000 members, making it one of the more remarkable evangelical growth stories of the early 2000s. The theological product being sold was a specific and assertive one: male authority, spiritual submission, and a hierarchical ecclesiology in which pastoral leadership was not merely encouraged but treated as spiritually obligatory. These are not incidental details. They are load-bearing walls in the structure of what followed.

The doctrinal emphasis on submission and authority is worth examining carefully, because it does not exist in a vacuum. When a leader builds an institution around the premise that questioning leadership is a spiritual failing, that institution becomes structurally resistant to the very accountability mechanisms that might catch a leader going wrong. This is not a coincidence. It is, in retrospect, a fairly efficient design for a particular kind of operation.

Investigations into Driscoll's tenure at Mars Hill documented years of bullying directed at staff and members, plagiarism in his published work, and misuse of church funds. These were not rumor or grievance; they were findings. The result was his resignation in 2014, after which Mars Hill Church dissolved entirely. Fifteen thousand members, multiple campuses, and a media operation, all dismantled because the man at the center could not be corrected.

The charitable interpretation of what happened next is that Driscoll spent time in genuine reflection, emerged humbled, and felt called to rebuild. The less charitable interpretation is that a man who had spent years preaching against accountability found the experience of facing it clarifying in a different way entirely. He founded Trinity Church in Scottsdale, Arizona, and resumed his ministry.

By 2021, former members and staff at Trinity Church were documenting a pattern of behavior that should have been, to any reasonable observer, immediately familiar. Allegations of spiritual abuse had surfaced again. Concerns about financial misconduct had surfaced again. Staff who raised those concerns had been fired, which is itself a data point worth noting: the mechanism for silencing internal correction was still operational and still being used.

The consistency across two institutions, two cities, and approximately a decade is the most analytically significant element of this story. Patterns of behavior this stable across different contexts and different personnel tend to reflect something structural about the individual at the center, rather than something circumstantial about the environments. Mars Hill was not uniquely toxic. Scottsdale was not uniquely enabling. Driscoll was the common variable.

It is also worth noting the specific theology that framed both institutions. A pastor who preaches that male authority is divinely ordained and that submission to pastoral leadership is a spiritual virtue has constructed, whether intentionally or not, a system in which his own behavior is insulated from normal correction. Congregation members who internalize that framework may interpret their discomfort with abuse as a failure of their own faith rather than as a reasonable response to genuine misconduct. This is not a minor feature of the operation.

Plagiarism, to be clear, is a separate category of concern from the spiritual and financial allegations, but it belongs in the same portrait. It suggests a relationship with attribution and honesty that has its own consistency. A man who will lift another writer's work without credit and a man who will misuse institutional funds without disclosure are both, in some fundamental way, operating from the premise that the rules applying to others do not apply to him.

The staff firings at Trinity Church deserve particular attention as a structural matter. In a healthy institution, employees who raise good-faith concerns about leadership conduct are protected or at minimum heard. In an institution organized around the suppression of dissent, they are removed. The removal of concerned staff is not an incidental management decision. It is the institution's immune system attacking the organism's own error-correction function.

Driscoll's story is frequently told as a rise-and-fall narrative, which is the structure that makes it most comfortable. Rise and fall implies a singular arc: ambition, achievement, hubris, collapse. What the Scottsdale chapter reveals is that the story is better understood as a loop. The fall from Mars Hill was not a conclusion. It was a reset, with new members who had not yet learned what the previous members had to learn the hard way.

The people who followed Driscoll to Trinity Church, or who found him there without knowing his history, are the quiet center of this story. They joined a church, which is an act of considerable trust. They placed themselves under a leadership structure that, by its own theological design, discouraged them from questioning that leadership. Some of them eventually did question it anyway, documented what they found, and came forward. That is not a small thing. That is, in the actual vocabulary of the tradition Driscoll claims, prophetic witness.

The institutional collapse of Mars Hill did not produce the institutional reforms that might have protected Trinity. The man who presided over one documented pattern of bullying, financial misconduct, and suppressed dissent was permitted, without apparent structural impediment, to establish a new institution and begin again. The question of who bears responsibility for that permissive environment, the broader evangelical ecosystem, the networks that platformd Driscoll post-resignation, the absence of any meaningful ecclesiastical accountability structure, is one the Atlantic piece raises and one that remains, as of this writing, largely unanswered.
subjectTitle: Founder, Mars Hill Church and Trinity Church
